title: 2018 AIME I Problems/Problem 5
problem: For each ordered pair of real numbers $(x,y)$ satisfying \[\log_2(2x+y) = \log_4(x^2+xy+7y^2)\] there is a real number $K$ such that \[\log_3(3x+y) = \log_9(3x^2+4xy+Ky^2).\] Find the product of all possible values of $K$ .
solution: Using the logarithmic property $\log_{a^n}b^n = \log_{a}b$ , we note that \[(2x+y)^2 = x^2+xy+7y^2\] That gives \[x^2+xy-2y^2=0\] upon simplification and division by $3$ . Factoring $x^2+xy-2y^2=0$ gives \[(x+2y)(x-y)=0\] Then, \[x=y \text{ or }x=-2y\] From the second equation, \[9x^2+6xy+y^2=3x^2+4xy+Ky^2\] If we take $x=y$ , we see that $K=9$ . If we take $x=-2y$ , we see that $K=21$ . The product is $\boxed{189}$ . -expiLnCalc
